metadata.name: Fiberglass
metadata.title: Fiberglass Settings
metadata.description: When laser cleaning fiberglass, I've found that starting with a gentle scan speed helps the process go smoothly. This composite material holds up well because of its strong, lightweight fibers that resist corrosion in tough environments like marine or aerospace settings. But watch out midway—its poor heat spread can cause localized damage if you push too hard, so adjust to multiple light passes instead of one heavy go. Tends to clean contaminants without scratching the surface when you keep the energy low and overlap spots just right. This way, you preserve its flexibility for applications in wind energy or construction.

author.name: Yi-Chun Lin

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Fiberglass surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength for optimal Fiberglass absorption and minimal transmission

spotSize.description: Optimal spot diameter for precision cleaning resolution

repetitionRate.description: High repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Scanning speed for efficient area coverage without thermal buildup

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage

dwellTime.description: Effective dwell time per spot for contaminant ablation

power.description: Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in composite cleaning to avoid thermal damage to polymer matrix; starting at 100W for light contamination (IPG Photonics specs for CL series cleaners; conservative for fiberglass melting point ~200-300°C).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds balance removal efficiency and heat input; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., 500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (2000-3000 mm/s); recommended for uniform fiberglass surface prep (industry standards from Laserax and Adapt Laser manuals).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred over ps/fs for cost-effective cleaning of organics on fiberglass; 30 ns minimizes HAZ in low-conductivity composites (laser physics studies in Optics & Lasers in Engineering, 2018, on polymer ablation).

repetition_rate.description: Moderate rates prevent heat accumulation in thermally sensitive fiberglass; higher rates speed cleaning but risk delamination (trade-off noted in Applied Surface Science, 2020, for composite applications).

energy_density.description: Fluence below fiberglass damage threshold (~2-4 J/cm² for polymers, higher for glass fibers); safe for contaminant ablation without substrate melting (ablation studies in Journal of Applied Physics, 2019, on epoxy composites).

spot_size.description: Smaller spots for precision on fiberglass textures, larger for broad areas; 300 μm balances resolution and efficiency (equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaners for industrial surface prep).

pass_count.description: Multi-pass for layered contamination removal without excessive heat; 1-2 passes typical for light industrial cleaning on fiberglass (case studies in Composites Part B, 2021, on laser surface preparation).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage and avoids gaps/striping on uneven fiberglass surfaces; 50% optimal for energy overlap without redundancy (process optimization in International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, 2017).
